articles.title: Kneejerk opponents wonder why they don’t get early notice
articles.narrative: This opinion piece criticizes local activists in Albany, NY, for opposing a proposed $7 million bus terminal project. The author argues that the opposition, led by a county legislator and a community leader, is a "NIMBY" (Not In My Backyard) reaction, primarily objecting to the project's location and the timing of community input rather than its merits. The article highlights the project's necessity, citing the impending closure of the current Greyhound station due to other construction and the need for a modern, equitable transportation hub for bus riders. The author suggests that the project, which has been developed through extensive collaboration among various government and economic development agencies, will serve a vital public need and improve regional connectivity.

data_points.notable_features: The project will replace a former McDonald's, a failed grocery store, and a concrete expanse, serving as a modern, welcoming 'front door' for Albany.
data_points.project_notes: The project is in line for funding from the Hochul administration and is being developed through collaboration between Advance Albany County Alliance, Albany County, the city of Albany, Empire State Development, the Capital District Transportation Authority, and regional bus carriers. It is urgently needed as the current Greyhound station will soon be unavailable due to other construction.

articles.title: Summit clears violations, but City Hall’s enforcement playbook is the real story
articles.narrative: This article is a multi-transaction digest covering several significant real estate stories. Summit Properties is working to clear housing code violations in a 5,100-unit rent-stabilized portfolio it acquired, facing scrutiny from City Hall and tenant unions. David Werner acquired One Dag Hammarskjöld Plaza for $270 million, securing a $250 million loan from JPMorgan and planning $60 million in upgrades. Additionally, a penthouse at Two Trees' One Domino Square went into contract for $7.8 million, setting a new price record for the building.

data_points.project_notes: Buyer plans an additional $60 million investment for upgrades to address the building’s 30 percent vacancy.

articles.title: How New York’s proposed ban stacks up in states’ fight over private listings
articles.narrative: New York is poised to enact legislation that would require brokers to add sale and rental listings to a public-facing website in a timely manner after marketing begins, unless a seller signs a disclosure agreement. This move follows Washington state's recent ban on exclusive "pocket listings," which prohibits agents from marketing properties to exclusive groups without making them publicly available. The debate over private listings is intensifying in states like New York and Washington, framed as a fight for access amidst housing and affordability crises, with other states like Wisconsin, Connecticut, Illinois, and Hawaii also considering similar measures. The New York Attorney General's office is also investigating Compass for antitrust concerns related to its merger with Anywhere Real Estate, as Compass has been a vocal opponent of rules requiring MLS uploads and has developed its own private exclusive network.

articles.title: NYC’s top deals: CSC Real Estate buys Yorkville office for $64M
articles.narrative: CSC Real Estate has acquired a nine-story, 73,300-square-foot office building in Yorkville for $64.1 million. The seller, an affiliate of Perlbinder Realty Corp., had owned the property for several decades. Additionally, Midwood Investment & Development purchased an 18,600-square-foot retail property in the West Village, and the Center for New Jewish Culture acquired the 61,400-square-foot Union Temple building in Prospect Heights for $17 million.

data_points.address: 210 East 86th Street, New York City, NY
data_points.project_notes: This article is a digest of multiple transactions. The primary focus for data extraction is the most expensive commercial sale.

articles.title: T2 escalates lenders’ clash over distressed Great Central Brewing property in wake of 1901 Project groundbreaking
articles.narrative: A foreclosure battle is escalating over the distressed Great Central Brewing Company properties in Chicago's Near West Side, an area experiencing significant development interest due to the adjacent 1901 Project. T2 Capital has filed a $2.1 million foreclosure lawsuit against an adjacent lot at 1745 West Walnut Street, naming developer David Avram. This action follows Harvest Commercial Capital's earlier $4.7 million foreclosure filing against the main brewery building at 221 North Wood Street. T2 Capital is seeking a joint sale of both the real estate and brewing infrastructure, aiming to maximize recovery in a market where industrial properties are under pressure from redevelopment.

data_points.project_notes: The T2 Capital debt stems from a $1.85 million compromise deficiency note to settle a previous $12 million foreclosure in 2023. The developer defaulted on the workout agreement by missing the first payment in March 2025. The properties are located within the Kinzie Corridor Planned Manufacturing District, an area protected by zoning laws.
